epigraph.text: For we know in part and we prophesy in part.
epigraph.quote: The population is an abstraction; the sample is a reality.

content.text:
Statistical inference involves using sample data to draw conclusions 
about populations. The key distinction is between parameters (fixed 
but unknown) and statistics (random and observable).

content.title: Parameter and Statistic
content.content:
A **parameter** is a numerical characteristic 
of a population. It is typically unknown but fixed.

A **statistic** is a numerical characteristic 
of a sample. It is known (once the sample is taken) but varies from 
sample to sample.

content.text:
Different samples from the same population yield different statistics. 
This **sampling variability** is not 
error—it is the natural variation inherent in random sampling.

content.title: Sampling Variability
content.problem:
Suppose the mean height of adults in a city is $\mu = 170$ cm. 
Three random samples of $n = 50$ might give:

- Sample 1: $\xbar_1 = 168.3$ cm

- Sample 2: $\xbar_2 = 171.2$ cm

- Sample 3: $\xbar_3 = 169.8$ cm
Each sample gives a different estimate, but all cluster around $\mu = 170$.

content.title: Sampling Distribution
content.content:
The **sampling distribution** of a 
statistic is the probability distribution of that statistic, considering 
all possible samples of the same size from the population.

items: What values the statistic can take
The mean and spread of the statistic across samples

marginNotes.text:
Give a portion to seven, or even 
to eight, for you know not what disaster may happen on earth.
